CURRENT AFFAIRB. -

A reader has sent us a copy of the book of a panto-
mime now being performed in Dublin. The back cover
contains a page advertisement of a motor garage in
Molesworth Street, Dublin. This garage is run by a
My. Phibbs, who makes the announcement ‘‘ Scotch
mechanics only employed.” What do the TIrish
mechanics think of this boycott? ~We wonder what
would happen if a similar firm in BEdinburgh put up a
notification that only Irish meclmnics_we}‘e employed ?
We suppose the Edinburgh people, indignant at the
flaxing boycott of their countrymen, would let the firm
find its customers where it found its mechanics.

A correspondent writes:—*‘ Now that we have Vigil-
ance Committees all over the country engaged in the
clean Press crusade, I would suggest to the various

committees to fight for another long-felt want, i.c., the
clean post-card.  The best way to stop the circulation
of the filthy post-card is to tackle the Post Office autho-
rities and insist on their carrying out the law as laid
down in the Post Office Guide—that is, to detain
obscene, - ete., post-cards and Christinas cards, n_nd
prosecute the sende: A notice in every Post Office
window warning the senders of filth that they are liable
to prosecution and six months’ hard if detected wop](l
stop the circulation in a very short time. During
Christmas time those filthy cards were all over the
country in their hundreds, thousands, perhaps mil-
lions. ;

Is the P.M.G., Mr. Herbert Samuels, a playboy ?
The Post Office circular No. 2025, issued before Christ-
mas, has an announcement that we suppose was
expected to fill the hearts of the officials with joy and
nerve them for the Christmas pressure. In future,
under certain circumstances, they are to receive Vale-
dictory Letters from the P.M.G.!" Well, of all the silly
things we have ever read in an official document this
thing about absurd Valedictory Letters is the silliest.
Here is the whole thing in full:—

‘“ VALEDICTORY LETTERS.

‘* The Postmaster-General has pleasure in announc-
ing that he has decided to issue a Valedictory Letter to
members of the Post Office staff, established or unes-
tablished, who, on and after the 1st of January, 1912,
retire on the ground of age or ill-health, or resign with

atuity, after not less than fifteen years of satisfac-
ce.
e Valedictory Letter, which will be signed by
the Postmaster-General, will be in the following form:

“ Sir (or Madam),

*“ On the occasion of your retirement from the Post
Office I desire to express to you my appreciation of
the faithful service you have rendered te the State
during a period of more than years.

g o
*“ Yours very truly,

*“ The letter will be engraved with an ornamental
border, and will be suitable for framing.

** Instructions are being issued to Postmasters and
others concerned for their guidance in making recom-
mendations for the issue of the letter.””

Has St. Martins le Grand taken leave of its senses?
We suppose in future the famiiiar phrase * walking
papers "’ will give way in Post Office circles to some-
thing like: *“ You'll get your valedictory letter, me boy,
if you don’t mind yourself!’’

It must be pleasant to get the push out as a result of
the process of favouritism in the Post Office when the
pushed one will get a *‘ Valedictory Letter ** to *“ Sir (or
Madam) *: and the letter will be *‘ engraved with an -
ornamental-border and will be suitable for framing.'
Surely this is a temptation in the way of weak sirs or
madams: they may pretend to be in ill-health in order
that they may get the push and the valedictory letter
before their time! There is considerable humour and
some literary ability amongst the Post Office staff in

5l

Ireland, particularly that portion of the staff that is
passed over, or shoved aside, in favour of ‘‘ Dumps
from Great Britain, so the P.M.G. may regeive a few
valedictory letters by way of Valentines in the near
future.

What about the filling of the positions consequent on
the new Insurance Act? Are the Nationalists
< and Catholics to be left out in the cold? We
would like to keep an eye on this matter, « for
in these days when Home Rule is “rembling
in the balance, -and when enemies of it are
stopping at nothing—mnot excluding truth—in their
attempts to work up a No-Popery passion against it
many ‘‘ Idolaters,”” tamer than we are, may be
inclined to be dumb whilst the Freomasons and the like
are working things in their own interests. The Labour
Exchange, we understand, will have a good deal to say
in the selection of one section of the staff of the new
Insurance Act army. Now, the Divisional Officer for
Ireland of the Labour Exchange is a Protestant, the
Assistant Divisional Officer is not only a Protestant,
but, in addition, an Englishman; the Lady Supervisor
is a Protestant, and of the three or four assistant
managers all are Protestants but one.  There were
three "lady registiiation clerks, one of whom was a
non-Catholic: the non-Catholic lady clerk is now one
of the assistant managers, and the two ‘‘ Idolaters
are still registration clerks. These facts leads us to be
rather suspicious of the exercise of the patronage of the
Labour Exchange in Ireland in connection with the
staffing under the new Insurance Act.

One of the Commissioners is a Mr. Kinnear, a non-
Catholic insurance expert. It is notorious that the
insurance industry-—largely a dumped industry—in
Ireland is a non-Catholic preserve. Mr. Kinnear him-
self is a non-Catholic, and all his young insurance
friends are, we assume, mostly, if not exclusively, non-
Catholics. We suppose hordes from the Blue Schools
and the Smyly Homes are sniffing after posts under
Mr. Kinnear, and we warn the country not to let the
mere Irish Catholics be edged out. =~ The Unionists
tried to make Party capital out of the imposition of
this Act on Ireland, and now that it is an Act we sup-
pose they will try all they know how to staff it and
keep Papist Paddy out in the cold.

We are still a green people. We suppose we would
be much greener had ‘the LEADER not been established :
but the LEADER’S eleven years’ fight notwithstanding,
we still have to admit that our people are green. The
Sourfaces have the ear of the Big Brother, nay, they
have to a great extent the ear of Ireland, for Ireland
where it is not sour is mainly tame. The tune the old
Bull lives on is some variation of Dan, Dan, Danny
McCann. The variations are unending, and one, or
more, is always on the programme. The question of
tolerance in Ireland, as we know, is tolerance for the
persecuted and down-trodden Catholics, but the com-
posite pack who have the ear .of the Big Brother keep
up the lie that the question is one of tolerance for the
non-Catholics. Are the ‘ minority’’ in Ireland a real
‘minority ? We have more than once lashed the ‘‘majo-
rity”’ for their tameness, hoping to stimulate them
into some human and righteous indignation. Bub
though we blame our tame people, we are not blind to
the main facts. They are only a majority from one
point of view. It is not raineis to say that probably
no people but the Irish Papists would have grit enough
to put up the long drawn-out fight that tho Irish

The real fact is that Irish Catholics are a minority
of the working entity—Great Britain and Ireland. The
Treemasons in Ireland and such like have the ear of
Greab Britain. From Dan, Dan, Danny MecCann on-
wards they are playing into that ear. So vigorously
are they playing that we cannot even recount the re-
cent tunes. There was some alleged case in Cork, and
some fellow named Kingston or Kingstown was ex-
ported to do the gramaphone over in England. But
whatever little thing, real or alleged, happens that
may be represented against Irish Catholics, the majo-
rity take it up—that is the so-called and mis-called
*“ minority’’—and play it for all it is worth in England.

The many cases of bigotry and persecution on the
part of the Sourfaces get little further than the pages
of the LEADER, which only circulates amongst the Trish
at home and abroad—that is, circulates amongst the
minority in the Great Britain and Ireland entity. Mr.
James Barry, now of Castlerea, bought a chemist’s
establishment in Greystones, and being a Catholic, he
was most shamefully boycotted in that very grey and
sour encampment. He detailed his case in the ‘‘ Free-
man’’ of last Triday (December 29, 1911), and we
wonder that the * IFreeman’ published it. It is &
plain, authenticated case of black, sour, premeditated
boycotb of a chemist merely because he was a Catholic.
It concerns an injustice to the minority—the Catholics

Our many tame Catholics are disheartening, but in
the New Year let us admit that in any other country
-they probably long since would have become non-
Catholics.. A tame Catholic is personally o poor crea-
ture, but the type in Ireland symbolises somewhat the
man‘in the Tnglish jingo picture with the blood on his
face and the handkerchief round his temples: ho is
very weak, but life has not -yet departed: and he
may recover to fight again.  We hope the Four
Courts Battalion, judges, K.C.’s, and plain B.L.’s will
be grateful to us for saying so much.

Still withal, the Catholics of Ireland might at the
present time be expected to put up a more manly front
to the *‘ Inish Times”—a paper edited by a ‘‘ saved '
Healy—and to all the other crowds of the S-urfaces.
The London ‘‘ Morning Leader’” is an Euglish Liberal
or Radical paper, and is therefore very much a non-
Catholic paper. In its issue of December 27th it had
a leading article on ** Catholics and Home Rule,”” and
a friend in Cork sent us a clipping of it—for we never
bother with English papers unless they are sent to us
by corresponder We quote the first paragraph of
the leading article in the London ‘ Morning Leader” :
. _Very littlo has lately been Lieard of the religious question
in Ireland from Ireland itself. But it is already playing a largo
part, and preparatioas are in hand to make it play a still larger
one in the British coastituencies. As regularly as a Home
Rule Bill, or any extensioa of seli-government to Ireland, looms
in sight, the ears of English, Sco and Welsh Nonconformists
are Incessantly filled with assert that the establishmen
a legislature in Ireland must mean the ascendancy ov
other interest in the country of the clerical representativi
a highly-organised, powerful, and far-sceing Church.
stock-in-trade of the Inglish campaign is that the priest is all-
powerful, and that he is thirsting for Home Rule to set his foot
on the neck of the Protestant population. Hgving been in more
or less constant use for thirty years, the menace has worn a
little thin. But an attempt is plainly going to be made to give
it fresh verisimilitude in coanection with a Papal decree which
the Dublin Daily Express unearthed last week, and the Dublin
correspondent of the Times announced yesterday would form
the subject to-day of an appeal to the “ whale electorate of
Great Britain.” As the correspondent in question is the editor
of the Irish Times, ho is in a position to make his impressive
prediction good ; and a trax rom his leading articlo was
duly circulated by an agency last night.

So Healy, the non-Catholic Healy, Editor of the
““ Irish Times,” is also Dublin correspondent-of the
“ Forger ”’ of England. No wonder Pensioner Yeats,
Horace Plunkett, the Rev. Hannay, and the I.A.0.S.
crowd are white-haired boys in the columns of the anti-
Trish *“ Trish Times.”” Might we suggest the following
degrees of comparison :—A bigot; a damn bigot; a Pro-
testant Healy.

Cork has had its meeting against the dirty papers.
Tt hadinot only a meeting, but a procession. All-for-I.
is a faction in Cork, but all against the ‘* penny dirties
has apparently united the people. We were afraid that

. the effects of the deplorable campaign. of Screeching
William would have militated against. Cork taking a
good;.  united stand in the campaign against the
““dirties,” and no doubt the evil of faction 1s account-
able.for the delay in Cork falling into line. But it is
very satisfactory that such a fine procession and meet-
ing were held last Sunday. Numbers of bands turned
out in the procession, and we believe bands are little
short of everything in Cork movements.

The Lord Mayor characterised the trade in _the
“* dirties’” as ‘‘a foul trade.”” Foul indeed it is, and we
hope shortly to have such a healthy state of public
opinion in Ireland that decent men would be ashamed

to be seen speaking to anyone engaged in
this foul and dirty trade. One cannot think
without indignation that a section of people for
a paltry, a very paltry, gain, should place

that gain before the moral welfare of the nation.  As
Father Tracy well said in his letter to the meeting:—
*“ Nothing was better calculated to arouse public indig
nation than that any persons, for motives of personal
gain, should attempt to sap the foundations of morahty
in a people entrusted with the pure traditions that
gather around the name of Ireland.’’

We gather from Father O’Regan’s speech that 120
newsagents in Cork have signed the pledge not to sell
dirty papers, and that none of the newsboys were sell-
ing the objectionable papers. Some of the newsagents
who signed the pledge evidently treated it as some
people in Cork and elsewhere unfortunately treat pledges
against drink—they broke it. The public procession
and meeting may have a strengthening effect upon
them. Father O’Regan said :—

As to why the public meeting was called—in their schemo
it was said that a public meeting would be held if thought
necessary. Unfortunately it had become necessary, for an
uncomfortable reason. Their work was not all smooth sailing.
Many newsagents, as ho had said, had signed the pledge, but
some unfortunately and disgracefully had broken it and gone

behind the committee’s backs and sold those ugly papers.

“F. F.” writes=—" Dear Sir,—You say in last
_week’s issue, ‘the native speakers who won’t speak
“and the Guaelic League quibblers have a lot to answer
for.” T fear that opinion is but too commonly shared.
The care of the national language is really a national
concern, but what should be everybody’s business seems
to be nobody’s business. To say that native speakers
who won’t speak, ete., have a lot to answer for is an
easy way to shirk responsibility for the perilous condi-
tion of the national language. Why, the Irish lan-
guage is beset by principalities and powers, of which
the very persons who are constantly blaming the native
speaker form no small portion.” The native speaker finds
the official language up against him at every point, even
to the huxter’s bill” He finds it a necessity for body
and soul. He has to meet the priest, the doctor, the
rate collector, the buyer, the seller, the lender, the
employer. Not one in a hundred of these will speak
to him in his own language. He must send his chil-
dren to an English school. The language they speak
in schocl they will speak out of school. Apparently
old Henry the Eighth grasped that fact, but it seems
to be beyond the grasp of his vietims of our generation.
In shert, the native speaker is being left with nobody
to speak to. How can he be expected to stem the
tide, or even to make the effort, when all above him
seem utterly unconcerned ? Who is there to save him,
or to aid him, except the Gaelic Leaguer, quibbling or
otherwise ? Where do the rest of the community come
in? Where the educationist, the politician, the eccle-
“siastic, the publicist, the man of means, the man of
learning ? They are the people in power, and the very
least the native speaker ought to expect from them is
official recognition of his language in and out of school.
Have they nothing to answer for, or is the poor native
spealker expected to rise superior to them all 2"

We do not see any inconsistency in saddling the
native-speaker who doesn’t speak with his due share
of responsibility and at the same time admitting the
difficulties by which he is beset. Surely it is many
times more difficult for an English speaking Ireland to
Lecome Irish speaking—and that is the main problem
—than for people who can speak Irish to speak it. Re-
proving those capable of speaking Irish for not speaking
it, or for not speaking it to a far greater extent than is
their habit, is not an attempt to take their share of
responsibility from those non-native speakers who are
not doing their duty. The native speaker is not the
only one who finds Finglish the official language, and yet
many of those who have to speak to their doctors, rate
collectors, clergymen, shopkeepers, and so forth, have
made plucky attempts to learn the language. — The
native speaker has not to learn it against cruel odds, he
only has to open his mouth and talk it when, as a man
of patriotism and spirit, he may reasonably be expected
to do so. Surely it is nob attempting to make a scape-
goat of him to say so much. If he has to send his
children to an Iinglish school, so have non-native

* speakers, and yet Trish Treland has, in face of the ad-

mitted difficulties, started out to Irishise Treland. The
attitude of ““ F. It.,”” it seems to us, is merely one of
throwing up his hands in despair and giving up the fight.
Surely it is unjust to the native speaker—we hape it is;
anyway—to represent that he is without grit and that
all the national spirit and five is gone out of him, so

THE MELODY UNHEARD.

No nobler hymns could fervid poet sing
Than, Freedom, one to thee—

When through each stricken soul should hopeful ring
““ Arise for Liberty!”

Quick-beating hearts and flashing eyes should show
Unquenced was hope’s bright flame,

And deathless honour would forever glow
Around that singer’s name.

To trumpet one grand pean of glorious song
Athwart the world’s dull soul,

That ever swelling high, should sweep along
Its theme from pole to pole,

Of man'’s true brotnerhood, of faith, of God:
To chant that hymn—and die—

Were worth the highest niche in fame’s abode,
'Twere immortality!

The whispered word that calls hope light again
To some poor outcast’s eye—

That calms the heart whose life was one long pain—
That teaches man to die;

That murmurs faith where but despair was known—
Where want’s dark shadows fall— 2

Unheard on earth, it swells around God’s throne,
The grandest note of all!

PUTTING THE CO-OPERATIVE CART
BEFORE THE HORSE.

HE fact that the marketing and handling of at least
three of the principal agricultural products of this
country, viz., butter, eggs and poultry, have hitherto
been conducted in a primitive and unprogressive
manner is due more to the want of uniformity in the
production of those commodities than to any other
cause. These industries, being practically non-
existent for about half the year, could not be expected
to attract the attention of our best business men
with capital to invest, with the result that, although
comparing favourably in bulk with other exports,
such as bacon, beef, live stock, and horses, the minor
products, as they are sometimes deseribed, have earned
an unenviable reputation for inferior quality and bad
handling in the British markets, while our Irish bacon
and ham, as well as live stock, rank amongst the
finest agricultural products of the world.

Incidentally, the collection and export of the articles
referred to have had.to be conducted as a side line,
and, unfortunately, have been, for the most part, in
the hands of the least progressive class of our country-
men. Obviously the first duty of a sympathetic
Government Department or a patriotic benevolent
society should be to endeavour to secure uniformity
of supply and quality, and it is still more obvious that
the multiplication of gombeen men, whether in the
shape of co-operative or proprietary concerns, could
not have the desired result. It may be said, of course,
and with some degree of truth, that increased pur-
chasing competition would naturally induce increased
production, but results from this cause have been
insignificant, and have been more than counter-
balanced by the disorganisation of trade arising from
over competition. This process, too, even if tolerably
successful, would be expensive and ill-advised, and,
in fact, has been found so by the co-operative societies
interested. The original gombeen man wisely, and
with sound business instincts, adopted the export of
butter and eggs as a side line, with his main business
to rely on during the lean half-year, and, being better
capitalised and managed, soon made his own terms
with the “intruder,”” who had to live on his * profits’
during the slack season. Trading losses were natur-
ally incurred by the new and old order, but the injury
inflicted. on the latter would be comparatively small.
The former, to save the situation, out-gombeened the
gombeen man, having discovered that the supposed
gold mine in the business was a myth, and that there
was nob room for two where only one existed hitherto.

Meantime suppliers soon found themselves in the
position to make their own terms. Inferior and un-
saleable goods had to be accepted to prevent customers
calling next door, and the fact that the co-operative
sharcholders were under no obligation to supply their

BLACKROCK (CO. DUBLIN).

l CONF.ESS to a feeling of sorrow when I read the
- Iiditor’s description of Blackrock as a ** quiet
.residential suburb.’’ 3

I_hupgeu‘ to live in Blackrock, so I ought to know.
It is neither *‘ quiet,” * residential,” nor yet is ib
5 5“‘{“‘"{";“.” in the ordinary sense of the term.

Quiet,” ah! me, think of the quictness of the aver-
age Irish town, the dead atmosphere that surrounds
it, th\b deadens yourself as you come into it, no life,
no vim.  Suburban! horror of horrors, that infernal
idea imported from Fngland ; long lines of red villas—
villas, mind you, then long lines of—more villas, all of
a stereotyped dissimilavity, that tires the eye and mys-

.tifies the stranger looking for ** Klm View.”

I may be narrow-minded and parochial, but I look
on my native place as a model Irish town. There have
been many avticles in.the LeApER strongly criticising
many of our Irish towns, so were it only for the sake
of variety, a panegyric of one of them may read well.

Blackrock, then, is a town, that is to say, it has a
personality of its own, just the same (only more so)
as if it were fifty miles from Dublin. It and its inha-
bitants are quite different from the surrounding town-
ships, curious to say, being very generally of Wicklow
extraction.

It is (very much) on the main road to Wicklow, and
you hit Bray before you know it from the 'Rock.” It
was to be sure in olden times, the outpost of the
O’Byrnes, of Wicklow, in their five hundred year war
with Norman and English Dublin, and to this day we
have so many Bymes in the 'Rock that we have to
give them nicknames—no offence meant to the great

an and none taken—such as the Grizzly Byrne, the
o Byrne, Shoulders Byrne, Spit-out Byrne, and. so
n

A

Otherwise, we are of no great antiquity in Blackrock,
ust a few hundred years, but, like the United States,
e have all the more veneration for ancient things, and
he *‘ Cross of the Rock,”” raised to its present position
ome years ago by our workingmen, of themselves and
y themselves, is an object of respect to all of us,
1ough it be only a boundary stone.

The land of the town itself is owned by the ‘‘ big
eople”” of Blackrock, and the “‘ lords of the soil ’ are
rish born and reared. This may account for our inde-
endence and initiative, for I believe we started most
hings, nationally speaking. ~ We had an Industrial
ssociation long before the Gaelic League thought
bout it, and we are all so used to buying Irish manu-
acture that it goes without saying almost. We were
rained to it twenty-five or thirty years ago.

The practice of displaying our own language (minus
he grammar) in name plates at street corners was first
introduced in Ireland at Cross Avenue (bothar trasna),
and the man who paid for them out of his own pocket
(Dr, Heron) was one of the first vice-presidents of our
thirtcen years’ old Gaelic League Branch.  Thirteen

years! It has Irish in every school in the parish, and
(among other great deeds) wrote and produced the first
play ever written in the Irish language (‘‘An Fiacloir,” :.

We have a National Registration Association: thirty-
five years old, a Workmen’s Club with a splendid pre-
mises of its own, and ever so many other clubs of high
and low degree, two bands, a hurling club (the only
one in South Dublin), a company of players (bless
’em), and, it is whispered, an Orange or Freemegon
Lodge, but the last hides itself from fame. Not to
speak of an industrial school, orphanages, colleges, a
Blackrock paper, a St. Vincent de Paul Conference,
penny banks, friendly societies, ete., to no end, all
established or worked by Blackrock people. Drunks
are very rare, and we have closed five pubs. in as many
years, leaving one pub. per 1,000 population.

There are four flourishing industries in the town,
employing over one hundred and fifty hands—Hosiery,
a laundry, horticulture and coach-building, and I must
not forget what the ‘‘ clever people’’ here term our
fifth industry, the making of M.P.’s, at which we have
trained several expert workers, exporting a finished
article every year or so, and no lack of raw material
for future expansion.

If we had a decent harbour, I have no doubt, we
would cap the enormous fishing wealth of Dublin Bay,
buf the one we have is small and in private hands, I
believe, though many a Viceroy landed from England
at it. We had a herring-curing industry here not so
many years ago.

We have, of course, a Carnegie library (we had a
library and reading-room of our own half a century ago,
and until lately), and (I do not know how) a Technical
School with a flourishing Inish class, and (again, of
course) a Town Hall, which contains our celebrated
Council that had the unique distinction of being pre-
sided over by a lady chairman for one session, and of
counting three M.P.’s among its members during an-
other. ~ The Suffragette idea, however, being new,
even for Blackrock, we abandoned the former arrange-
ment, temporarily, I have no doubt.” Besides being
gallant, the Council take a great interest in the work-
ingman, and have provided over 200 workingmen'’s
dwellings, which per cent.-of the population exceeds
that of any other Urban District in Ireland. Shortly
we expect to have no slums.

Lastly, we have a Penal Days chapel (in Booters-
town), the oldest church in which Mass has been con-
tinually celebrated in Dublin, .or indeed in Ireland, for
all T know to the contrary. Not so very long ago it was
a ‘‘ Mass House "’ for the half of South County Dublin,
and to this day looks like anything but a church from
the road. At each side of the High Altar, you may
note the secret doors (now, of course, no longer secret)
for the priest to escape, in case of molestation.

DEBUSSY IN DUBLIN.

P HROUGH the energy and pluck of our distin-
" guished fellow-citizen, Mr. Thos. Quinlan, we are

to have fine performances of great masterpieces, from
 Tristan,” the acme of musical attainment, to the
‘* Tales,”” pre-eminent in its own peculiar genre.  In
the selection of his programme our Ivish impressario
has shown great artistic discrimination, and amongst
woihs new to Dublin he has included ** I.'Enfant Pro-
digue ” of the French composer, Debussy, who, escap-
ing the fatal magnetism of the German giant has suc-
ceeded in breaking loose from tradition and sounding an
absolutely uew note in modern opera, has given pre-
eminence to his nation in this sublime form of art.
The work of the great ones in the realus of art has been
generally distinguished by a spe ol evolution, early
work influenced by contempor s or predecessors, a
middle period in which peculiavities and personality
began to develop, and then g later period in which a
geeat soul unburthens itself of the intimations of the
divitie which it has been privileged to receive, and
carves, paints, or sings immortally, and whether in
sculpture, peinting, or music, this tendency with many
of the great masters has developed—with the painter,
until outline would seem to be lost in a blaze of gor-
geous colour; with the musician, until the wealth of
melodic material and ornamental device would seem to
clathe iorm with a very cloud of sound and leave it but
as dimly visible as Gothic glories seen through twilight
hazes ; with the sculptor, until the highest e pression of
his genius to the uninitiated might scarcely be distin-
guished from a huge block hewn from its parent quarry.
If this evolutionary tendency be a passport to the
company of the immortals, Debussy is already one of
* the divinities, being distinctly a master, with manners
from his early pianoforte works of little value, except
inasmuch as they tickled the ears of the throng to that
wonderful tonal conception, *‘ Pel:as and Melisande,”’
which overturned many preconcei ideas with. regard
to opera and showed the French musician as thorough-
going a revolutionary aytistically as his compatriots
have ever been politicall Quinlan, with that
cultivated artistic instinct for the great art works which
marks him out as a virtuoso amongst i S, un-
doubtedly has deep down in his_heart the intention of
some day unfolding in Dublin the tonal and poetical
beauties of Maeterlinck and Debussy at their greatest ;
but meanwhile he wishes to attune ouwr ears to the
delightful vagueness of the Debussy idiom by producing
an opera which, containing as it does in the Prelude,
and later in the work in the Cortége and Air de Danse,
tne germ of future greatness is yet lilely to be popular
with those who worship at the shrine of the conventions
raore on ‘account of fugitive reminiscences of Gounod
and his contemporaries than of metcoric intimations of
future triumphs in the kingdom of tones. In this little

work the purely instrumental portion could have only
been written by Debussy, whilst the vocal part miga
have been done by many others with a talent for cloth-
ing common emotion with suitable melody. To me the
differences betweaen the vocal and instruniental portions
of this work would seem to be as great as sunshafts on
summer streams compared to the midnight rays in
regions of etornal snow. The names of Debussy and
his compatriot D’Indy have been coupled as leaders of
the modern I'rench school of music.  This coupling
might have held if Debussy had never progressed beyond
the purely emotional, if exquisite, vocal contents of
the prodigal, but since he has set subtleties to music
and invented a tonal medium so admirably suited for
the expression of the psychological in drama as to make
the melodic idiom as inevitable as the spoken word, he
must be allowed to go further into the sacred heights
end unattended.
Debussy has been credited with, speaking somewhat
ireverently of Wagner's use of the Leit Motif, and
refers to the Teuton Titan bringing his characters on the
stage, each presenting his visiting card. Yet our com-
poser could not altogether free himself from the fasci-
nation of the ILeit Motif idea, and in the work under
consideration there are some interesting evidences of -
the desire to gain musical synthesis by the use, chary
though it be, of the very means which gives vitality to
the music of the Ring or binds together the components
of Wagner’s immortal musical monument. The Pre-
lude of ** I.’Iinfant Predigue’ is a foreshadowing of the
contents of the work built on material from the proces-
sion music and a beautiful melodic phrase which after-
wards appears as an accompaniment to Lia's recitative,
** Along the Silent Shore.”” A characteristic Debussy
chord ushers in the recitative and air, ‘“The sad years
roll by,” in which Lia bewails the absence of her son
in music which adequately expresses the poignancy of a
supreme sorrow. A recitative follows, in which Simeon,
the spouse of Lia, reproves her for her continued
despondency, and is characterised by that pride and
severity which conquers if it does not altogether stifle
grief. This recitative leads in turn to what may be
considered the most beautiful portion of the work, and
which seems, I have already said, to foreshadow
future greatness, containing, as it does, music of much
tonal and rhythmic interest. It is followed by a recita-
